title: Bilabrella cribbiana
taxonomy: Bilabrella cribbiana is classified within the family Goodeniaceae, a group of flowering plants commonly known as the goodenia family. This family is part of the larger order Asterales, which includes various flowering plants such as asters and sunflowers. Bilabrella cribbiana is an endemic species found in Australia, and its classification is an important aspect of understanding its unique characteristics and relationships within the plant kingdom. The classification of Bilabrella cribbiana is further specified within the genus Bilabrella, which is a distinct group of flowering plants. The classification of Bilabrella cribbiana provides valuable information for botanists and researchers studying the plant's evolutionary history and ecological role.
uses: Bilabrella cribbiana is reported to have various medicinal uses, including anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties. The plant has been traditionally used to treat various health conditions, such as skin infections and wounds. Its leaves have been used in folk medicine to treat fever and other respiratory problems. Bilabrella cribbiana's roots are believed to possess antiseptic properties, making them useful in treating infections. Further research is necessary to confirm the plant's medicinal uses and potential health benefits.

Its antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties may help to reduce the severity of conditions such as acne and eczema. Additionally, the plant's antioxidant properties could aid in protecting the body from oxidative stress, potentially reducing the risk of chronic diseases like cancer and heart disease. More research is needed to confirm the extent of its health benefits and potential uses.
properties: Bilabrella cribbiana is a plant that has been traditionally used in Australian Aboriginal medicine.  It is believed to possess antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. The roots and leaves of Bilabrella cribbiana contain compounds that are thought to contribute to its therapeutic effects. The flavonoids present in Bilabrella cribbiana may also exhibit antimicrobial and free-radical scavenging properties.
